title: LightGBM: A Highly Efficient Gradient Boosting Decision Tree

tldr.text: It is proved that, since the data instances with larger gradients play a more important role in the computation of information gain, GOSS can obtain quite accurate estimation of the information gain with a much smaller data size.

title: LightGBM: A Highly Eﬀicient Gradient Boosting Decision Tree

tldr.text: The new GBDT implementation with GOSS and EFB LightGBM is called and it is proved that, since the data instances with larger gradients play a more important role in the computation of information gain, GOSS can obtain quite accurate estimation of the information gain with a much smaller data size.

title: A Novel Scheme for Mapping of MVT-Type Pb–Zn Prospectivity: LightGBM, a Highly Efficient Gradient Boosting Decision Tree Machine Learning Algorithm

title: Full-real-life Wind Power Prediction based on Gradient Boosting Decision Tree

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: The following paper fields have been elided by the publisher: {'tldr'}. Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/ITOEC63606.2025.10968639?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/ITOEC63606.2025.10968639, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

journal.name: 2025 IEEE 8th Information Technology and Mechatronics Engineering Conference (ITOEC)

abstract: Wind energy is highly random, volatile and uncontrollable. Therefore, in order to ensure the reliable and efficient operation of wind farm power generation connected to the grid in a full-real-life environment, accurate wind power prediction based on weather forecasts is crucial. This paper uses a learning algorithm based on a gradient boosting decision tree (XGBoost algorithm and LightGBM algorithm) to develop two models to study the power prediction effect in a full-real-life environment. Both models are trained using ECMWF weather forecast data and 19 months of wind farm power generation. Preliminary experiments show that the XGBoost algorithm is slightly better than the LightGBM algorithm in terms of accuracy, but the LightGBM algorithm is much shorter than the XGBoost algorithm in terms of training time. Since data is updated frequently in a full-real- life scenario and the model training time requirement is short, a model based on the LightGBM algorithm is selected to verify the power prediction effect of a wind farm in a full- real-life environment. In a full-real-life environment, the ECMWF weather forecast is input into the LightGBM model to perform ultra-short-term, short-term and medium-term forecasts on wind farm power. The full-real-life evaluation standard adopts the industry grid-connected rules. The simulation results of the full-real-life environment confirm that the learning algorithm based on the gradient boosting decision tree can not only be used for ultra-short-term, shortterm and medium-term wind farm power forecasting, but also has excellent prediction results in the ultra-short-term and short-term. At the same time, more accurate weather forecasts are more important for power forecasting.

title: Predicting Live Weight for Female Rabbits of Meat Crosses From Body Measurements Using LightGBM, XGBoost and Support Vector Machine Algorithms

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11720723, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

tldr.text: It was aimed to predict the BW using body length, chest girth and width of the waist for rabbits of the maternal form of Hyla NG with over 95% accuracy for both train and test sets and the BL was determined as the most explanatory variable on body weight.

abstract: ABSTRACT Prediction of body weight (BW) using biometric measurements is an important tool especially for animal welfare and automatic phenotyping tools that needs mathematical models. In this study, it was aimed to predict the BW using body length (BL), chest girth (CG) and width of the waist (WW) for rabbits of the maternal form of Hyla NG. The standard rabbit‐raising practices were applied for the animals. A highly efficient gradient‐boosting decision tree (LightGBM), eXtreme gradient‐boosting (XGBoost) and support vector machine (SVM) algorithms were evaluated and compared to the prediction of BW. The coefficient of determination, root mean square error and mean absolute error values were used as comparison criteria. The results showed that LightGBM, XGBoost and SVM algorithms were well fit for the BW using the biometric measures with over 95% accuracy for both train and test sets. The BL was determined as the most explanatory variable on body weight.
